data.title: Xiao Feng and Vitalik had a fireside chat: The next phase of Ethereum applications.
data.description:
On the afternoon of April 22nd, Xiao Feng, Chairman of Wanxiang Blockchain and Chairman & CEO of HashKey Group, and Vitalik Buterin, co-founder of Ethereum, held a fireside chat at the main venue of the 2026 Hong Kong Web3 Carnival, focusing on the theme "Future Outlook – The Next Stage of Ethereum Applications." The following is a transcript of the fireside chat, with slight modifications that do not affect the original meaning.

Xiao Feng:Dear friends, welcome to the second annual fireside chat with Vitalik. We also held one last year. Would Vitalik like to say hello to everyone? ...>Xiao Feng:Dear friends, welcome to the second annual fireside chat with Vitalik. We also held one last year. Would Vitalik like to say hello to everyone?

Vitalik Buterin: Hello everyone! Xiao Feng: Hello everyone! Actually, before we came on stage, we just had a discussion about the combination of smart hardware and blockchain. I also know that in the past few weeks, Vitalik has met with many companies and entrepreneurs related to chip and smart hardware manufacturing. After hearing this, I was also curious: Why are you suddenly so interested in smart hardware and chips at this moment? How will your focus on these things intersect with Ethereum? I'd like to ask you to talk about this topic first. Vitalik Buterin: We've recently started thinking about many topics, besides Full-stack open-source security. Full stack refers to every layer of an application, including the application layer—if it's a website, then the browser is a layer, the operating system is a layer, the hardware is a layer, so there are many different layers. If any layer is insecure, then the entire system is insecure. We keep saying we want to do something secure that doesn't require centralized trust, but if we really want to do that, we need to care about every layer. I see many people saying they are ETH holders. So, how is the security of your ETH guaranteed? First, it's your wallet; second, it's your computer's operating system; third, if you're using a hardware wallet, there's another issue: how can you trust that your hardware wallet is secure? Additionally, there's been a topic of discussion in the last two years: everyone hopes that applications based on blockchain platforms can do more in the real world, which also relies on hardware. Therefore, whatever we do, the hardware layer is important. Xiao Feng: Yes. Security issues don't just exist at the Ethereum and L2 layers; in fact, in Ethereum's layer 2 protocols, hardware is also needed to accelerate certain calculations. After all, hardware acceleration is currently one of the means to solve performance problems. Perhaps today, when blockchain and Ethereum, as infrastructure, need to support more applications, hardware will also be needed to ensure security or provide better efficiency and performance. This also picks up where the previous forum left off. There's a topic I've noticed you've been discussing, but it hasn't resonated with many, and I think it might be a very important one. We know that blockchain and Ethereum still have a high barrier to entry for end-users. This includes not only wallets but also smart contracts, because not everyone has the ability to write a fully functional smart contract. You've also been mentioning Ethereum's simplification, which I understand to mean lowering the barrier to entry for Ethereum, making it easier for more people to use. This reminds me of the history of computers. The earliest computer operating systems were command-line based. Bill Gates created the DOS operating system, which was also command-line based. If current computer operating systems were still command-line based, I believe 90% of computer users in the world wouldn't be able to use computers, because it would be extremely difficult to learn. Then came browsers, and later graphical operating systems like Windows. As the barrier to entry lowered, computer operation became increasingly simplified, and more and more people used it. The biggest simplification came with the advent of mobile internet and the emergence of apps. We know that WeChat is an extremely complex system, but by packaging it into an app, you only need one finger to operate it. That is to say, you can operate a computer function or program with just one finger. We certainly look forward to Ethereum gradually being simplified, automated, and intelligentized, allowing more people to conveniently use Ethereum with just one finger. Could you elaborate on the paths you've mentioned and envisioned for simplifying Ethereum, and your ultimate goal? Vitalik Buterin: I think the word "simplification" actually has two meanings. The first meaning can be compared to the simplification of the iPhone. You have a phone; it seems very simple to the user—you know what it does by pressing a button—but behind the screen is a very, very complex system. The second meaning is to simplify even this very, very complex system. Both are actually important. Why is the second important as well? Because if a system is too complex, it means that only a small number of people can participate in the core development process. You can say that the system is theoretically trustless, but if the core of the system is very complex, and only 50 people know how it works, then the whole world still needs to trust those 50 people. Therefore, an overly complex system cannot be a trustless system. Another point is user experience. I think there are many differences between the command line and the GUI. One difference is that the GUI is more user-friendly, but the command line also has its advantages. A very important advantage is that 50 years ago, in the command-line era, there was a Unix philosophy. The Unix philosophy has many components, one of which is: we should make very simple programs. A user's operation may simultaneously call many different programs. For example, the user's input will be input to the first program, the output of the first program will become the input of the second program, and the output of the second program will become the input of the third program—this is the concept of a "pipeline." This concept has an advantage. In the GUI era, a point of criticism or dissatisfaction was what some called digital monopoly or digital feudalism. The natural consequence of a GUI is that you have a UI controlled by a single company; you live within that company's microcosm, and your digital life is controlled by that company. The Unix philosophy is completely the opposite; it has many different programs, each made by a different company, and they can all interact with each other via command line. Now we've reached the third step: AI. The Ethereum Foundation is starting to focus more on AI. Last year, we formed the Kohaku team, a wallet development team. Last month at EthCC, Kohaku released its first beta version, and they said they'll release a final version soon. But we all feel that the current concept of a "wallet" might disappear in two years; the future belongs to AI. Therefore, we need to start thinking about AI wallets and how to make them more secure. We're also thinking a lot about AI user interfaces. AI is essentially a command line, except you just talk directly to the computer. Xiao Feng: Using natural language. Vitalik Buterin: Yes, using a natural language command line. In this third era, it can be said to possess the advantages of the first two eras simultaneously: the advantages of the command line, allowing many people and different small programs from different companies to operate interoperably, while also providing a good user experience. If we do it well, we can combine the advantages of the command line and the advantages of a GUI. If done well, the results will be excellent, but we need to work hard. Xiao Feng: Command-line operations can be used by numerous computer programs developed by many developers, while an app is a single application that integrates everything. From a consumer welfare perspective, it's ideal for consumers to easily use various programs developed by numerous developers; this maximizes consumer welfare. If this isn't possible, simplification is necessary for usability, resulting in an app. Behind every app are many other developers, but they are integrated onto a single platform. Now, Vitalik envisions a potential "regression" with the advent of AI, a return to command-line computer manipulation. Of course, this command line won't be the one we learned before, but rather one using natural language—you'll say a sentence, and the computer will invoke countless programs to help you achieve your goal. I'm really looking forward to doing things like this with the help of AI and natural language. I recall that when we talked about simplifying Ethereum, this topic was already being discussed at the 2016 Ethereum Developers Conference (Devcon) in Shanghai, such as tools or techniques for simplifying smart contract writing. I remember that two different technical development teams discussed formal verification at Devcon 2016—using formal verification methods to verify all applications on Ethereum, including various smart contracts, to find bugs and make them perfect smart contracts. Now that AI is here, is it possible that AI combined with formal verification could allow any individual, even without programming skills or being a computer engineer, to build a smart contract for their own purpose or needs, and that this smart contract would indeed be bug-free, not vulnerable to hackers, and run perfectly? To put it another way: formal verification has been discussed for ten years. If we add AI to it today, could it enable everyone here, regardless of their computer programming skills, to write their own smart contracts and run them on Ethereum? Vitalik Buterin: Yes, formal verification is a very important topic now. Writing smart contracts is a crucial part. We talked about it a lot in 2016 because of the DAO attack that year, which made everyone realize the importance of smart contract security. But this year, Claude Mythos came out, which surprised many people, even frightened them. As I understand it now, Claude Mythos is indeed more powerful than previous AIs, but some of the claims are exaggerated. We can't just look at Claude Mythos now; we need to look at the trend—Claude Mythos is much better than AI a year ago, next year's AI will be much better than Claude Mythos, and the ability of AI to find various vulnerabilities in 2029 might be terrifying. Right now, I believe the only way is to use AI, formal verification, and many more methods to obtain truly secure code, code truly free of serious vulnerabilities. Now, because of AI, this is almost possible, and because of AI's ability to find various vulnerabilities, it's also necessary. Currently, our team developing the next version of Ethereum is setting many goals for the next version of Ethereum's PoS consensus. First, faster. The finality time will be reduced from the current 16 minutes to 16 seconds, 8 seconds, or even less. Second, quantum resistance. Since last year, everything being considered and developed considers quantum resistance necessary. Third, greater decentralization and security. They have many needs, and one of the things being done now is to prove the security of many parts of the next version of consensus using formal verification methods, including quantum-resistant signatures, quantum zero-knowledge proof technology stacks, and many other complex things like Recursive STARK. Two years ago, everyone thought that formally proving such complex things was completely impossible, and would never be possible. But now, because of the rapid development of AI, these things have become possible. Therefore, AI is becoming an increasingly important part of L1 core development. Writing smart contract layers is also very important, as various hacking incidents have become increasingly serious recently. In fact, on-chain contracts have been relatively fine in the last two years; more problems have arisen in the UI layer and off-chain layers. AI can also help. If you use AI, think about last year's Safety tool; the Safety tool is a UI tool. If everyone used Local AI and Safety, that incident would not have happened. If done well and correctly, AI can replace third-party UIs and help with security, but AI also has many security issues of its own, which is something we are considering. In the smart contract layer, Vyper, the UI's second programming language for writing smart contracts (Solidity was the first), now supports formal verification, and Solidity's capabilities will continue to improve. This tool is not perfect and cannot immediately solve all problems, but its capabilities combined with AI are indeed very powerful. Xiao Feng: We just discussed how formal verification can empower everyone to write their own trustworthy smart contracts. You also mentioned two topics I'd like to discuss with you: quantum computing and quantum-resistant computing. We've seen Google publish its own paper, and some commentators have said that Google's paper points out more risks than... Therefore, the Ethereum network and Ethereum technology have many security advantages. I think the Ethereum L1 DeFi ecosystem should also make a choice. Our transformation goal is Low-risk DeFi, to create decentralized and secure DeFi. No matter what happens, no matter which company goes bankrupt, no matter which people or institutions are hacked, your assets are still safe. I think this is achievable. This is a choice, and we need to choose to do this. Xiao Feng: Vitalik just mentioned that security is not just a technical issue, but also a matter of willingness—are you willing to write a very secure program? This is the first layer. The second layer, AI can bring a lot of security, but there's a saying among programmers now: "mountains of shit code." AI creates so much code that it's hard to tell which parts are correct and which are wrong. Of course, AI can also be used to find many bugs and prevent vulnerabilities in DeFi. Furthermore, Ethereum has always emphasized that if the so-called blockchain trilemma (decentralization, security, performance) is accepted, then although Ethereum is developing layer-two protocols like L1 and L2, Ethereum L1 only emphasizes decentralization and security, and will not attempt to improve performance through other methods. Ethereum has no intention of increasing transactions per second because a perfect solution to this trilemma has not yet been found. Therefore, the performance issue is left to the second layer, to be solved by application scenarios; Ethereum itself is only responsible for decentralization and security. I consulted cryptographers, and they use zero-knowledge proofs to build cross-chain bridges. At least for now, zero-knowledge proofs are a good solution to the cross-chain asset interoperability problem encountered by DeFi. We've already discussed quantum computing and quantum-resistant computing, which are security issues. Zero-knowledge proofs, and fully homomorphic encryption (discussed by developers at Devcon Shanghai 2016)—both are privacy-preserving technologies. Of course, they have slightly different focuses: zero-knowledge proofs focus more on proving the truth or accuracy of something after encryption; fully homomorphic encryption allows others to calculate the same result as in the plaintext. One focuses on proof, the other on computation, but both are performed in ciphertext. When we develop Ethereum applications, including HashKey, there's a HashKey Chain, which is a Layer 2 layer of Ethereum. When we recommend Ethereum blockchain solutions to organizations with very strong requirements for compliance and privacy protection, they all have very strong privacy needs. Of course, on a public blockchain, data is 100% permissionless to be seen by others, which is precisely one of the inherent characteristics of blockchain. However, Ethereum users do have very strong privacy protection needs, thus requiring the use of privacy-preserving and privacy-computing technologies such as zero-knowledge proofs and fully homomorphic encryption. We've been talking about zero-knowledge proofs for ten years, and fully homomorphic encryption for ten years. Can these technologies, as they've developed, meet the application-oriented needs of Ethereum? Can they be adapted to large-scale commercial applications? What stage have we reached in our development? Do we still need to wait for a certain point in time? Vitalik Buterin: I think zero-knowledge proofs are quite mature now, and many applications are using them. We've spent ten years developing many development tools, and now, with the addition of AI, it's very simple. FHE (Fully Homomorphic Encryption) may be slightly behind ZK, but it's developing rapidly, and more and more applications are starting to use FHE recently. ZK and FHE have different advantages and disadvantages. ZK's advantage is that it's completely trustless; you don't need to trust anyone, verification is verification. FHE's disadvantage in terms of trust is that it always requires a private key for decryption; there always needs to be a person or company possessing the private key, and only through them can you obtain the result of the FHE computation. You can't restrict them to saying, "You can only get the result of this computation, not the result of another computation," which is a disadvantage of FHE. However, FHE's advantage is that it can perform computations and can combine data from different users. ZK allows a user to perform computations using their own private information, verify the results, and then send the proof to another person or on the blockchain. FHE has other characteristics. The third technology is called obfuscation, which theoretically combines the characteristics of ZK and FHE, but it's currently very slow and practically impossible to implement. Theoretically, we know how to do it, but in practice, it takes about a year to run, so it's still in the research field. If it could be achieved, I think it would be excellent. However, combining ZK and FHE is already quite feasible. Xiao Feng: This is extremely important for the integration of blockchain and AI, because any AI application involves computation. But if there's only AI computation, a problem arises: if AI computation isn't encrypted, I believe its adoption will be relatively slow. Whether it's AI or a standalone blockchain, the data is open and transparent to users. Yesterday I gave an example: hospital medical data is extremely valuable, but the requirements for privacy protection are also the highest. If AI research methods could use hospital data, it would obviously greatly improve human welfare. However, without addressing privacy protection and the ability to perform computations in an encrypted manner, I believe no hospital would dare to share this data with researchers—whether for drug development or medical solution development. Encryption is certainly possible, for example, homomorphic encryption, ZK, and zero-knowledge proofs are all feasible. But if you only combine AI with privacy-preserving computation technology, it essentially becomes a Web2 business model: you need to negotiate with the hospital, sign agreements, obtain permission, and pay. I believe this is extremely difficult for hospitals, from a "token factory" perspective, to commercialize. However, if we add blockchain—AI + privacy computing + blockchain—and transform it into a permissionless and trustless platform like Bitcoin or the Ethereum network, all the hospital's data becomes AI tokens. Since they are AI tokens, using blockchain, they are permissionless and trustless, and anyone can use them. When you consume a hospital's token, you pay the hospital; the hospital doesn't need to know who the other party is. In this way, from the perspective of a "token factory," the hospital's business boundaries will be greatly expanded. In my opinion, these three technologies need to be combined to create enormous commercial value. The next topic I'd like to ask about is Ethereum's L2. Ethereum's solution to the L1/L2 layering is the L1/L2 layering. I believe the L1/L2 is a mathematical problem, and challenging a mathematically verified L1/L2 is technically very difficult. But this problem is not unsolvable; the solution is to delegate the performance issues to another layer. What are your thoughts on the future development of L2 and the relationship between L1 and L2? What do you think is the best relationship for the future? Vitalik Buterin: Thank you, Mr. Xiao, for raising many important topics. The topics of healthcare and FHE are particularly interesting. We recently had a project that combines almost all the points we just discussed. This project is a device that can display air quality indicators. Actually, there's a big problem with air quality data: through this data, we can know when someone is in a room, when they aren't, who is in the room, what they are doing, and much more, including through AI. So what we did was: directly take that data from the device/hardware, encrypt the information using FHE, and also add ZK encryption. You need to know that the information comes from good hardware, but you don't know when it came from. FHE can protect the content of information, sending it to a server where scientific research can be conducted to guide improvements in air quality and human health. This requires ZK (Zero-Knowledge Base), FHE, secure hardware, and small, high-performance hardware—all are necessary. So it's a problem of cryptography, hardware, medicine, and applications—it's present at every layer. The project we're working on now is quite interesting. AI and FHE are also very interesting. If we can incorporate LLM reasoning into FHE or zero-knowledge proofs, we can expand the applications we can make. Incorporating L1 reasoning into FHE means your phone, IoT devices, computers, and all devices can securely use the most powerful models. If we can do LLM proofs, we can create applications, such as writing smart contracts—theoretically, we can write smart contracts using natural language. We can prove the content of certain articles, what happened according to LLM judgments, and then send some coins to a specific account. We all know that several companies are working very hard to optimize FHE and ZK for AI, and I especially hope they succeed. Regarding L1 and L2, there has been a significant change in the last two years: Ethereum L1 is now willing to scale more than before. Why? Because with ZK technology, we can finally separate "block creation computation" and "block verification computation." This has led us to rethink the blockchain trilemma for the first time. The trilemma didn't include the ability to separate execution and verification through ZK, but now that capability is available, there are plans to significantly increase the gas limit this year and further next year. However, there will still be many things that L1 cannot do. The ideal L2 is not simply "L1 + more scaling"; I find building a larger EVM rather pointless. What's more interesting is starting to think about what applications need—and what aspects of those needs can L1 not do? What other applications require services that L1 doesn't provide? You can provide those services; that's what L2 is all about. I think there will be many new L2s that are completely different from previous L2s and won't be just larger copies of the EVM. In fact, current L2s are already starting to move in this direction. Arbitrum has been saying "We are more than just the EVM" for a long time. If they can successfully follow this path, it will be the best approach for Ethereum and for them. Xiao Feng: On the one hand, with new technologies like ZK, the blockchain trilemma can be addressed. As the foundational L1, Ethereum's performance can be significantly improved with modern technology, and the level of the trilemma may gradually rise. Of course, no matter how much performance improves, decentralization and security will remain the same; it's just that new technologies can solve performance issues that are better and higher than before. This is the first level. The second level concerns the relationship between L1 and L2: L2 should be more application-oriented, rather than doing what L1 has already done. There's no need to reinvent the wheel, and it's not necessarily commercially viable. Application-oriented, different applications have different personalized needs, which L1 might not consider because they are too specialized. L2 needs to consider and meet these personalized needs, including performance requirements. Some applications have higher performance requirements than L1 can meet, and L2 can handle these. I completely agree. I understand that the Ethereum Foundation updated its mission, vision, and values ​​about two months ago. Many of you here are Ethereum fans, and the outside world may not be very familiar with the Ethereum Foundation's new mission, vision, and values; indeed, I haven't seen anyone translate it into Chinese yet. Why did the Ethereum Foundation update its mission, vision, and values ​​recently? What kind of future Ethereum Foundation does the updated foundation aspire to be? Vitalik Buterin: In the past two years, there have been many significant changes both inside and outside the blockchain ecosystem. First, AI has brought about many changes. Within the blockchain ecosystem, many new chains have emerged, many new applications have appeared, and many applications are beginning to move towards greater centralization. We've seen many mainstream adoptions. However, within the Ethereum ecosystem, it's crucial to emphasize that we should do what only Ethereum can do. If decentralized communities only do what companies can do better, then we cannot pretend to be an HFT company, a bank, or a centralized exchange. We need to distinguish between two things: efficiency and decentralization, security, privacy, and trustlessness. The first point can be considered an advantage that benefits everyone in the ordinary world. The second point may seem useless at times—why think about it?—but it is still essential at crucial moments, and only Bitcoin and Ethereum can do those things. Other chains struggle to allow a single technical development team to create five truly independent development teams; it's simply impossible. Therefore, we should emphasize our own advantages—the things that only Ethereum can do on the blockchain. L2 can improve efficiency, applications can improve efficiency, and L1 should provide what only Ethereum L1 can provide. Furthermore, from the perspective of what applications and blockchain bring to the world, Bitcoin and Ethereum, as L1 blockchains, don't currently focus on efficiency. If you want efficiency, AI is currently providing significant efficiency improvements, and centralized finance can also offer substantial improvements. What can blockchain do for us? I can give a more philosophical example. In the EF documentation, we mention many philosophies, including censorship resistance, anti-capture, open source, privacy, and security. -webkit-text-stroke-width:="" white-space:="" background-color:="" text-decoration-thickness:="" text-decoration-style:="" text-decoration-color:="" line-height:="">, also mentioned soft sovereignty.

data.title: A Plain Language Explanation of OpenAI Images 2.0: What's Being Issued Isn't an Image, It's a Token
data.description:
Author: Silicon Valley Alan Walker

Afternoon Tea Time: Watching This Launch Event from Palo Alto

At noon on April 21, 2026, OpenAI held a livestream on its official website. Sam Altman personally took the stage to release a new product calledChatGPT Images 2.0, with the underlying model calledgpt-image-2. Several key facts: All ChatGPT users (including free users) can use it. Paid users (Plus, Pro, Business) unlock an additional mode called "Thinking"—the model "thinks" before drawing, can search and analyze uploaded data online, and can generate up to eight images with consistent character and style at once. API It's released simultaneously, and the model ID is gpt-image-2, supporting any aspect ratio from 3:1 to 1:3, with a maximum resolution of 2K. It's natively integrated into the Codex environment, requiring no separate API Key. Altman's original words on stage were, "This upgrade is like jumping from GPT-3 to GPT-5 in one leap." The opening line of the official blog post is worth mentioning here: "Images are a language, not decoration." Then, on Twitter, @OpenAI used a self-generated video as a demonstration in their April 21st post, with the caption simply, "state-of-the-art image model that can take on complex visual tasks." Below, Alan wants to break down eight things with you, things that I've been repeatedly considering over afternoon tea, things that most media outlets and Twitter KOLs haven't yet touched upon. Before the tea cools: Today's release isn't an image model. At 4 PM at Zombie Café, California Avenue is as usual. Just as I finished my second cup, the news feed exploded. TechCrunch, VentureBeat, The Decoder, Tom's Guide, Engadget, Axios—all released articles within the same hour. Ninety-nine percent of the headlines were just one of three sentences: "Text rendering is more accurate," "You can draw comics now," "Chinese characters no longer look like gibberish." These are all true, but they're all just fluff. Alan stared at the opening line of OpenAI's official blog for a long time—"Images are a language, not decoration." This isn't a literary statement; it's a strategic declaration. For the past three years, image generation has been an "add-on feature" in OpenAI's product line—DALL-E 1 was a toy, DALL-E 3 was an insert, and GPT-Image-1.5 was a usable but not robust tool. Today, the positioning of gpt-image-2 has changed, from a "model for drawing" to "the foundation of the vision modality." Altman's statement during the live stream, "From GPT-3 to GPT-5," can be translated into plain language as: OpenAI is no longer treating image processing as a sideline; from today onward, it's part of the main trunk.

To understand everything I'm about to say, you must first grasp this basic judgment—Today's release is not a product update, but a repositioning of the product.

Look at the price first, don't look at the picture: $30 for one million tokens, an economy where images are stuffed into text

Most people watch product launches to see product features, but Alan watches launches by first looking at the Pricing page. ... This API pricing structure for Images 2.0 is the most signal-driven one he's seen in his twenty years in the industry. (The rest of the text appears to be a series of characters and symbols, possibly representing a font or image. A direct translation isn't meaningful without context.) agent="" ai="" language="" :="" fal.ai="" m="" fireworks="" nodeleaf="" img="" src="https://img.jinse.com.cn/7454819_image3.png" type="block" mpa-paragraph-type="title">

Did you understand? The image and text use the same unit of account—token.

Do you understand?

By the way, fal.ai launched its enterprise-level API on the same day, priced from $0.01/image (low quality) to $0.41/image (4K). OpenAI is holding onto the high-end market at $30/M output, letting infrastructure providers like fal, Replicate, and Fireworks compete in the low-end market—avoiding direct competition with low-end vendors themselves. This is a typical Altman strategy.

...

Eight identical images generated in one click:The advertising agency's revision bill was handed to the client

The most talked-about capability of Thinking mode is "outputting up to 8 images with coherent characters, styles, and scenes at once".

The most talked-about capability of Thinking mode is "outputting up to 8 images with coherent characters, styles, and scenes at once".

All the media are talking about comics, storyboards, and character consistency. These are all correct, but none of them hit the nail on the head. The nail on the head is this: For the past twenty years, the Achilles' heel of the global advertising agency industry's business model has been "variation fees." You're a brand, and you want to do a campaign this year. You approach WPP, Publicis, or Omnicom, and the agency tells you: We'll give you three Key Visual concepts; you choose one. After that, we'll create eight variations around that concept—a summer version, a winter version, a Xiaohongshu version, a Douyin version, a LinkedIn version, an Instagram Story version, an outdoor billboard version, and a WeChat Moments version. Every variation incurs a charge, every revision consumes time; this is the meat in the entire food chain of the creative director, art director, and post-production team. Today, Images 2.0 does this: A prompt gives you 8 variations that maintain character consistency. From the client's perspective, this isn't tech news about "AI creating comics." This is the old question—"Why did I have to pay variation fees to agencies in the past?"—which Altman answered with a prompt. WPP repeatedly touted its transformation into an "AI-native agency" in its financial report this year, Publicis acquired Sapient Razorfish to automate workflows, and Omnicom and IPG merged to talk about "scaling creativity." These narratives need to be rewritten from today onward—clients have a new weapon in their hands, and agencies can no longer use "labor-intensive" as an excuse to charge variation fees.

This point is something to wait for when we see this quarter's financial report; we'll most likely hear widespread lamentations.

This is something to look forward to; we'll have to wait and see what the financial report is like this quarter.

Drawing in Codex without API Key: The most overlooked flaw is in the engineer's tools

This is the most underestimated line in all the reports.

This is the most underestimated line in all the reports.

The Decoder article only briefly mentioned it: "In Codex, image generation will be available directly in the workspace without a separate API key." There was almost no discussion about it on Twitter. But this sentence is more significant than the previous two combined. You are an engineer. In the past— Drawing a UI mockup required switching to Figma. Drawing an architecture diagram required switching to Excalidraw. Drawing a Mermaid flowchart required embedding it in Markdown. Creating a banner for the README required opening Canva. Every time you said "I need to draw a diagram," it was something outside the workflow, requiring you to open another tab, another account, and another mental model. From today onward, your coding agent—Codex, Cursor, Windsurf, Continue, Cline, any tool that connects to the OpenAI API—can draw production-level visual assets within the same context window. No tool switching, no additional keys, no copy-pasting, no layout alignment required. This isn't just killing a tool; it's killing the very reason for the existence of the "engineer-side visual tools" category. Mermaid's Markdown charts, sustained by developer passion; Excalidraw's sketches, supported by minimalist aesthetics; Draw.io's enterprise marketplace; Figma Dev Mode's penetration into the engineer market—all of them must re-examine: Why do I still exist? This is a typical example of Altman "casually taking away a category." He didn't even emphasize it in his keynote. The official OpenAI post listed five languages ​​below this: Japanese, Korean, Hindi, and Bengali. Domestic media mostly translated it as "Chinese character rendering is better." Sitting on a Palo Alto, the accurate reading is: From today onwards, OpenAI is the most powerful design tool in the Chinese-speaking world—not just one of them. For the past two years, the Chinese characters on overseas models—Midjourney, DALL-E, and Stable Diffusion—have always appeared as gibberish. This is a fact, no argument. This is precisely the moat that has sustained the Chinese design SaaS industry for the past two years: GaoDing Design: Claiming 400 million users, specializing in one-click generation of Chinese posters. Chuangketie: ByteDance-affiliated, specializing in Chinese social graphics. TuGuaiShou: Incubated by ZCOOL, providing e-commerce visual templates. Meitu Design Studio: B-end product of Meitu XiuXiu's parent company. MasterGo: Lanhu's design tool, competing with Figma. These five companies combined have an annual revenue approaching 8 billion RMB. Their core value proposition—which I'll translate into one sentence—is "AI can draw Chinese characters correctly and lay out Chinese layouts correctly." Today, this moat has been breached by an American company using an API. And not by brute force—the official blog specifically emphasizes "rendered correctly but with language that flows coherently," meaning it's not just about placing characters correctly, but about allowing the characters and layout to blend naturally at the linguistic level. This is the effect of combining Thinking Mode with CJK training data. Several of our Chinese founder friends in the Bay Area are probably having an emergency meeting tonight. They either need to integrate with the OpenAI API as a distribution channel or find a vertical market to hide in; there's no middle ground. E-commerce visuals, social graphics, and brand promotional graphics—these three largest categories—have all suffered significant losses today.

QR code demo:The most underrated trump card of the entire press conference

There was a demo in Livestream that almost all the media glossed over, but only a guy named Leonardo Gonzalez on Substack pointed it out in his article "ChatGPT Images 2.0 Explained". The demo works like this: OpenAI had Images 2.0 do one thing—capture user feedback on the internal codename "duct tape" on LMArena, synthesize it into a poster with layout, and then embed a scannable, real QR code that redirects to ChatGPT within the poster. Take a moment to think about it. This is not an image generated. This is three things packaged into one product: Information Gathering (web search to capture social feedback) Layout Design (arranging the gathered information into a poster) Machine-Readable Encoding (embedding a scannable QR code to redirect to a real URL) In Alan Walker's words, "a picture" is no longer a visual endpoint, but an information container. This is a paradigm shift. What does this mean? This means that from today onwards: Posters are no longer just posters; they can be dashboards with real-time data. Infographics are no longer just designs; they can be interactive assets with link portals. Packaging design is no longer just packaging; it can be an identity marker with traceability, brand story, and social touchpoints. Business cards are no longer just business cards; they can be living information with resumes, portfolios, and contact details. Incidentally, OpenAI's internal codename for Instant mode is "duct tape." This codename itself reveals their product philosophy. They define what they're making as "the tape that holds together all the visual needs in the AI ​​ecosystem." This is the self-perception of infrastructure, not the self-perception of a tool. Infographic SaaS, data visualization tools, QR code generators, business card design apps—these used to be independent categories, but from today onwards, they are all just alternatives to a subset of Images 2.0. Visme, Piktochart, Infogram, Lucidpress—they all have to hold board meetings overnight.

How to view stocks:Adobe takes another hit, Canva's IPO story will be rewritten tonight

Break it down by magnitude.

Adobe (ADBE): Since the beginning of 2026, its stock price has fallen by 22%, and Morgan Stanley downgraded it from Overweight to Equal-Weight this year. Firefly's corporate narrative still has one card left—"professional workflow for the last mile of Creative Cloud." This card was directly stabbed today by Images 2.0's positioning as a "strategic design system." Analysts will likely lower their target price again within three months. Figma (FIG): Its IPO in July 2025 opened with great fanfare, but it has fallen by 66% in six months. Today, Codex's native drawing feature addresses the vulnerability of its "design tool + dev handoff" business line. Dev Mode was originally its tool for penetrating engineers, but today engineers no longer need that tool. Shutterstock (SSTK) and Getty (GETY): The inventory charting business was already half dead, surviving only on the narrative of "AI training data licensing." Shutterstock launched its official app on ChatGPT three weeks ago—but that's for distribution, not production. OpenAI today offered a self-developed production solution, passively lowering Shutterstock's bargaining position. Getty's class-action lawsuit is still ongoing, and the strategic value of the lawsuit itself has diminished today. Microsoft (MSFT) and Nvidia (NVDA): The winners are obvious. MSFT holds shares in OpenAI, and NVDA sells inference chips. However, these two have been priced in too many times, so there's nothing new about them. Canva: This company isn't publicly listed yet and is reportedly preparing for an IPO in Q3 or Q4 of 2026. Its narrative has always been "AI-powered design platform for the rest of us." Today, this narrative needs to be rewritten—because the "rest of us" is now directly drawn within ChatGPT, eliminating the need for Canva as an intermediate layer. Tonight, Canva's IPO team is likely rewatching their roadshow deck. Wix (WIX) and Squarespace (SQSP): Victims largely overlooked. The website building business used to rely on "design barriers"—attractive templates, reasonable layout, and font alignment. Today, these three barriers have been flattened by Images 2.0. The next step in website building is "one prompt, one website," and Wix and Squarespace's Elementor editor will become relics of the past. Pinterest (PINS): The most hidden loser. The market structure for visual inspiration distribution is changing—people used to find inspiration on Pinterest, but will now directly use ChatGPT to generate it. Long-tail DAU will be gradually siphoned off. A brief overview of startups: Midjourney uses a style engine, while Ideogram and Recraft only last 72 hours. Each company will be given a chance to survive or face certain death. Midjourney: A direct head-on collision, but its moat of artist users remains. It won't die in the short term. My suggestion is to immediately abandon the self-positioning of "we are a creative platform" and shift to "we are a style engine"—specializing in generating images with a clear aesthetic DNA that Images 2.0 can't do well. OpenAI excels at being versatile, multi-purpose, and workflow-friendly; Midjourney needs to do what it can't do well—"the kind of style that only Midjourney can create." Ideogram and Recraft: Their core selling point over the past year has been "accurate text rendering." OpenAI has taken that card from the table today. They must find a new narrative within 72 hours, or their next round of funding will be disrupted. Possible directions: Recraft will move towards vector graphics and branding systems, while Ideogram will move towards a designer toolchain. Both companies need to shift their focus. Leonardo AI, Krea, Playground: General image generator brands have all become commodities. They can only continue to compete in price wars. Playground has been struggling for the past two years, and today's events are a sign of acceleration. fal.ai, Replicate, Fireworks AI, WaveSpeed: The infrastructure layer is actually more comfortable. Who's the front-end developer doesn't matter, since the inference is all running here. fal.ai launched its gpt-image-2 enterprise API today, priced from $0.01 to $0.41—this is the standard strategy of "building another layer under OpenAI." This layer is safe in the short term, but in the medium term, we'll see if OpenAI truly pulls back the infrastructure to do it itself. Runway, Pika, Luma: short-term security, but one thing is clear—images are the atoms of video. After OpenAI tokenizes images, video is merely an extension of the time dimension. The next Sora 2 or 3 launch will likely repeat today's scenario. Until then, these three companies need to accumulate workflow depth and brand awareness. The longest list of victims. As discussed in Section 5 above, they either need to delve into distribution channels or find a vertical scenario that OpenAI cannot reach. Finally, let me mention one company I actually favor: any company that embeds the gpt-image-2 API into its own domain's workflow and can maintain the triangle of "professional knowledge + compliance auditing + corporate trust"—medical imaging, legal documentation, financial compliance, architectural design, and brand management. These areas present challenges that OpenAI cannot overcome: industry-specific knowledge, data compliance, and customer trust. OpenAI has smoothed out the underlying layers, making the value of the upper layers clearer—you no longer need to train your own models; you only need to focus on how your industry can best utilize this token.

This is the third cup of tea. The light on California Avenue outside the window is moving westward.

To summarize today's OpenAI presentation in one sentence: Today, OpenAI didn't just release an image model; it released a new visual token exchange.

gpt-image-2 This API, its generate call has only two lines: 

result = client.images.generate(    model="gpt-image-2",    prompt="your prompt here")

Or the simpler cURL:

curl -X POST "https://api.openai.com/v1/images/generations" \  -H "Authorization: Bearer $OPENAI_API_KEY" \  -d '{"model": "gpt-image-2", "prompt": "..."}'

Two lines of code, $30 One million tokens, eight images generated at once, accurate Chinese character rendering, native call within Codex. It looks simple, but it's actually a channel that bypasses all the middle layers of the visual creation industry chain of the past twenty years. The rest depends on who comes out on top and what cards they're playing next quarter.

data.title: Half of the GPT Image 2 team is Chinese; 13 people achieved legendary status in 4 months.
data.description:
GPT Image2 is trending online, but why is it so effective?

Research Leader Chen BoyuanRevealed: The underlying architecture has been completely reconstructed.

However, he refused to answer whether a diffusion model or autoregressive technique was used, only mysteriously describing it as a "general model" or "GPT in the image domain". A tweet from Chen Boyuan also revealed that, starting from GPT Image 1.5 at the end of last December, such a significant improvement was achieved in just four months.

Such groundbreaking achievements, the core team consists of only 13 people.

Gabriel Goh, the team leader, shared a family photo of the AI ​​team members.

A netizen in the comments section exclaimed: Why are they all Asians? ...>

Chen Boyuan: From Not Understanding Python to Research Lead

What exactly is the architecture of GPT Image 2?

OpenAI probably won't release it for a long time, but some clues can be seen from the academic experience of the core team members.

OpenAI probably won't release it for a long time, but some clues can be seen from the academic experience of the core team members.

Boyuan Chen is the team's Research Lead. He and another member, Kiwhan Song, shared the same advisor, Vincent Sitzmann, during their PhD studies at MIT. His representative work from his doctoral studies, *Diffusion Forcing: Next-token Prediction Meets Full-Sequence Diffusion*, was selected for NeurIPS 2024. This study proposes a novel sequence generation training paradigm, Diffusion Forcing, which combines token-by-token independent noise level diffusion with causal next-token prediction, integrating the variable-length generation of autoregressive models with the long-range guidance advantages of full-sequence diffusion models. During his internship at Google, he also co-authored SpatialVLM. By automatically constructing an internet-scale 3D spatial reasoning VQA dataset (10 million images, 2 billion QA pairs), this study endows visual language models with quantitative/qualitative spatial reasoning capabilities, enabling them to output precise numerical values ​​such as metric distance, size, and orientation from a single 2D image. This research applies thought chain spatial reasoning to the field of embodied intelligence. During his internship at Google, the instruction fine-tuning technique he developed was later adopted by Gemini 2.0. While participating in a research summer camp in high school, he didn't know the basic syntax of Python. It was Xia Fei, a senior researcher at Google DeepMind, whom he met there, who introduced him to the world of AI. Xia Fei invited him twice to complete high-quality internships at DeepMind. These experiences allowed Chen Boyuan to accumulate engineering experience in large-scale model training and provided him with valuable insights into the data requirements of multimodal systems. After graduating with his PhD, Chen Boyuan joined OpenAI in June 2025 and quickly became one of the five core members of the GPT image generation team, responsible for all training of the GPT image generation model. He is also a member of the Sora video generation team. In the demonstration, he created a poster for his hometown, Wuxi. Then he created a Korean poster for his teammate from Seoul and a Bengali poster for his teammate from Bangladesh. The text rendering in each poster was accurate and error-free.

Jianfeng Wang from USTC: Enabling AI from Raw Images to Understand World Knowledge

Jianfeng Wang, a PhD graduate from the University of Science and Technology of China (USTC), is responsible for another amazing capability in the GPT Image 2 team: instruction following and understanding the world. The old model depicts a clock that always points to 10:10, a feature found in almost all online clock advertisements. This is because watch manufacturers conducted experiments with psychologists, believing this helps stimulate consumers' desire to buy watches. He had the new model drawn in 2:25, 3:30, 9:10, and 7:45 scales, all with perfect accuracy. This is just the appetizer. More complex spatial layouts: apple in the center, cup on the right, book on top, camera on the left, basketball at the bottom. The model is executed with perfect precision. Before joining OpenAI, he worked at Microsoft for nearly nine years. During his time at Microsoft, he collaborated with the OpenAI team on DALLE-3. He has published numerous academic papers in the field of computer vision, with research covering areas such as image classification, object detection, semantic segmentation, and visual representation learning. His research has significantly improved his understanding of world knowledge, providing a correct understanding of the semantic content and functional structure of objects. At the end of his demonstration video, JianFeng Wang stated: "GPT Image 2 is eliminating the gap between your intent and the model's output. It truly delivers exactly what you want from the model." Yuguang Yang: Generating High-Precision Complex Infographics Yuguang Yang demonstrated the generation of infographics and PowerPoint presentations at the GPT Image 2 launch event. A 75-page GPT-3 paper was dragged into ChatGPT, and 7 slides were automatically generated. His experience is arguably the most diverse among the team members; each job he's held has been a cross-disciplinary move, but all have focused on machine learning. He earned his undergraduate degree in engineering from the Chu Kochen Honors College at Zhejiang University and his doctorate in computational chemistry physics and machine learning from Johns Hopkins University. His first full-time job was as a quantitative analyst. While a visiting researcher at Tsinghua University, he worked on reinforcement learning and control algorithms for nanorobots. Later, he conducted research on Alexa voice recognition at Amazon. He also worked at Microsoft on query understanding and retrieval for Bing search, and document understanding. After joining OpenAI in early 2025, he participated in the ChatGPT intelligent agent project, in addition to image generation. He introduced the infographic generation capabilities of GPT Image 2 on his personal account, saying it can save researchers a lot of time.

It also repeatedly reminds everyone that when making infographics, don't forget to select the thinking mode. ...>

From DALL-E to GPT Image 2.0

From the self-introduction of team member Kenji Hata, we know that GPT Image 1.0 is the image generation part of GPT-4o.

From the self-introduction of team member Kenji Hata, we know that GPT Image 1.0 is the image generation part of GPT-4o.

There is someone who has participated in the entire OpenAI multimodal research series since DALL-E.

He is Gabriel Goh, the team leader of GPT Image 2.0.

He joined OpenAI in 2019. His early research was more theoretical, focusing on interpretability and convex optimization, etc.

Starting with DALL-E, the focus gradually shifted to image generation. Seeing the research resume of another team member, Weixin Liang, reveals another corner of the technical background of GPT Image 2. His representative work during his internship at Meta, *Mixture-of-Transformers*, introduced modal decoupling MoE and decoupled attention, significantly reducing the computational cost of pre-training multimodal models. He received his PhD from Stanford University and his undergraduate degree from the Chu Kochen Honors College of Zhejiang University, though several years later than Yuguang Yang. Like Chen Boyuan, Weixin Liang joined OpenAI right after graduating with his PhD in 2025 and quickly became a core member of the team. Other members of the GPT Image 2.0 team include: Ayaan Haque, who previously worked at Luma AI and participated in the training of Luma's video generation foundation model, Dream Machine. Bing Liang, who worked at Google for over 5 years, participating in Imagen3, Veo, and Gemini Multimodal, will move to OpenAI in 2025 to conduct image generation research. Mengchao Zhong, an alumnus of Shanghai Jiao Tong University (undergraduate) and Texas A&M University (Master's), has worked as a software engineer at Pinterest and Airtable, and is responsible for engineering multimodal products at OpenAI. Dibya Bhattacharjee, a Yale University graduate, won a bronze medal at the 2015 IPhO and achieved the highest global scores in CIE A-Level Mathematics and Biology. Kiwhan Song, who joined last in October 2025, is not only a researcher but also a master of cue words on the team; many of the official demo images you see are his work. From the earliest DALL-E to today's GPT Image 2.0, this team has successively solved the problems of being able to draw, draw clearly, draw beautifully, and draw accurately. Despite significant talent turnover at OpenAI in recent years, OpenAI remains a company that continuously attracts diverse and talented individuals, welcoming cross-disciplinary collaborations and adhering to bottom-up emergent research principles. Starting as a small team, achieving breakthroughs leads the company to allocate more resources until it changes the world.

One ​​More Thing

Once, GPT-4o's image generation, mimicking the style of Studio Ghibli, swept the world.

Now, members of the GPT Image 2.0 team have all changed their own avatars to this bizarre style.

So what are the clues for this art style? The team members have also revealed them.

So what are the clues for this art style?

Use my photo only for identity. Redraw me as a very simple surreal Japanese sticker-style caricature: long thin neck, small deadpan face, minimal black outline, flat  light coloring, almost no shading, very few facial details, simplified hair shape, lots&nb sp;of white space, plain white background, slightly awkward and funny. Ultratall 1:3 image.

data.title: Tesla's BTC holdings remained unsold in Q1; Iran has not yet decided to hold talks on Friday.
data.description:
Headline

▌Tesla did not sell its Bitcoin holdings in Q1 2026

On April 23, Tesla disclosed that it did not adjust its Bitcoin holdings in Q1 2026, still holding 11,509 BTC, worth approximately $880 million at current prices. Due to the drop in Bitcoin prices from approximately $90,000 to $68,000, the company recorded approximately $173 million in after-tax fair value losses on its digital assets. Despite this, Tesla's overall financial performance was mixed, with earnings exceeding expectations but revenue slightly below market expectations, driving the stock price up by approximately 4% in after-hours trading.

▌Iran has not yet decided to hold negotiations on Friday

According to Iran's Tasnim News Agency: Iran has not yet decided to hold negotiations on Friday.

As of press time, according to CoinGecko data: BTC price is $78,266.46, a 24-hour change of +2.5%; ETH price is $2,377.69, a 24-hour change of +2.1%; BNB price is $637.72, a 24-hour change of +1.1%. SOL price is $86.96, a 24-hour change of +1.3%; DOGE price is $0.09579, a 24-hour change of +0.6%; XRP price is $1.43, a 24-hour change of +0.2%; TRX price is $0.3298, a 24-hour change of -1.0%. The price of WLFI is $0.07829, with a 24-hour change of -0.9%; the price of HYPE is $41.19, with a 24-hour change of +3.5%. Policy: The Russian State Duma passed the digital currency bill in its first reading, giving the central bank control over market access and transaction regulation. On April 22, the Russian State Duma (lower house of parliament) passed the "Digital Currency and Digital Rights Bill" in its first reading, marking a key step towards the legalization of crypto assets in the country. According to the bill, the Bank of Russia will become the core regulatory body for the cryptocurrency market, responsible for issuing licenses, approving or prohibiting related transactions, and defining the legality of transactions. The bill proposes to recognize cryptocurrencies as "property," but explicitly prohibits their use as a means of payment domestically; the ruble will remain the sole legal tender. However, under Western sanctions, crypto assets can be used for cross-border trade settlements, including service payments and intellectual property transfers. Furthermore, the bill allows Russian residents to legally invest in crypto assets through licensed institutions, but will implement an investor tiered system, setting tests and annual investment limits for ordinary investors (a suggested cap of 300,000 rubles). Initially, only high-market-cap mainstream assets such as Bitcoin and Ethereum will be allowed to trade, with the central bank creating a whitelist. The bill is expected to be formally passed and take effect no later than July 2026. However, some lawmakers and banking professionals have criticized its overly strict regulation, potentially affecting market activity and even causing funds to remain in the gray market. Simultaneously, supporting legislation also proposes to introduce criminal penalties, with illegal cryptocurrency transactions punishable by up to seven years imprisonment.

▌Trump and the White House Deny Setting a Deadline for Ceasefire Extension

On April 23, Trump, in an interview with Fox News, stated that reports of a 3-5 day window for extending the ceasefire were false. Regarding Iran's seizure of ships in the Strait of Hormuz, Trump stated that they were not American vessels and that he would continue to monitor the situation. As for when the war would end, Trump said there was no timetable and he was not in a hurry. "People say I want to resolve this quickly because of the midterm elections, that's not true," Trump said, adding that the administration hopes to secure a good deal for Americans. Trump also said that blockades frighten them more than bombings; they have been bombed for many years, but what they hate is the blockade. Once those oil wells stop producing, sometimes it's a permanent closure. Trump expects the Iranian foreign minister to still be there when negotiations resume. Earlier, Fox News and Axios reported that Trump had extended the ceasefire by 3-5 days, rather than indefinitely.

... In response, White House Press Secretary Levitt issued a statement saying that Trump did not set a deadline for extending the ceasefire with Iran, and that the claim of giving Iran 3 to 5 days to respond was untrue. The US-Iran ceasefire was originally scheduled to end today; Trump said talks could resume as early as the 24th; Iran denies this. According to CCTV on April 23, the originally scheduled temporary ceasefire between the US and Iran ended on the evening of the 22nd Eastern Time, which was the morning of the 23rd Beijing Time. However, the planned new round of negotiations between the US and Iran has been difficult to advance. On the evening of the 21st, Iran formally refused to attend the second round of negotiations originally scheduled to be held in Pakistan on the 22nd. On the same day, Trump stated that he agreed to extend the ceasefire with Iran, but would continue the naval blockade against Iran and maintain a state of combat readiness. On the 22nd local time, Trump stated that a new round of US-Iran peace talks might resume as early as the 24th, and said that there was currently "no timetable" for ending the conflict with Iran. Iran, however, denied holding new negotiations.

Blockchain Applications

▌Google Cloud Launches $750 Million Fund to Help Partners Accelerate Intelligent Agent AI Development

Google Cloud announced a $750 million fund to provide new resources and incentives to its partner ecosystem to help accelerate the transformation of their mutual customers using intelligent agent AI. The fund is open to global consulting firms, systems integrators, software partners, and channel partners. (Bloomberg)

▌Tencent and Alibaba in Talks to Invest in DeepSeek at a Valuation of Over $20 Billion

April 22, according to The Information: Tencent and Alibaba are in talks to invest in DeepSeek at a valuation of over $20 billion.

▌Robinhood Ventures Invests $75 Million in OpenAI

Robinhood Ventures has invested $75 million in OpenAI through its recently launched publicly traded fund, Robinhood Ventures Fund I. This is reportedly the fund's largest deal to date. Sarah Pinto, president of Robinhood Ventures Fund I, stated that including Robinhood as an investor provides a way for companies considering an IPO, including OpenAI, to connect with future shareholders.

▌SBF's Early $200,000 Investment in Cursor, Currently Worth Approximately $3 Billion if Not Sold

On April 22, SpaceX announced a partnership agreement with AI programming startup Cursor, which granted SpaceX the option to acquire it later this year for $60 billion, or pay $10 billion to advance the collaboration.

It is reported that Cursor developer Anysphere completed a $400,000 Pre-Seed funding round in April 2022, jointly invested by Alameda Research and Heroic Ventures. Alameda invested $200,000 in Anysphere, acquiring approximately 5% of the shares. This stake was sold at the original price during FTX's bankruptcy liquidation; had it not been sold, its current value would be approximately $3 billion. Cryptocurrency ▌Coinbase Q1 Report: Crypto Market in Bear Market or Late Bear Market Phase, Bitcoin Significantly Undervalued On April 23, Coinbase Institutional and Glassnode jointly released the "2026 Q1 Crypto Market Chart Analysis Report," with the following key points: 1. 75% of institutional investors and 61% of non-institutional investors still believe Bitcoin is undervalued. “The crypto market remains influenced by certain important factors, such as regulatory developments and the rise of agency AI, but their importance has diminished against the backdrop of a volatile global macroeconomic environment.” 2. Currently, 82% of institutional investors and 70% of non-institutional investors believe the crypto market is in a bear market or late-stage bear market, a significant increase from 31% and 36% respectively in December. Meanwhile, expectations for Bitcoin dominance are shifting towards a more “stable” state, reflecting more the impact of the macroeconomic environment. 3. In Q1 2026, the BTC supply that moved in the past three months decreased by 37%, while the supply that hadn't moved in over a year increased by 1%. This indicates reduced short-term trading activity and more tokens being locked up by long-term holders. 4. Finally, despite a more volatile period for the crypto market, cumulative inflows into Bitcoin ETFs remain near all-time highs. Overall, the report suggests a more cautious market sentiment, but the underlying fundamentals of the crypto market continue to improve.

▌BlackRock's BTC Holdings Reach 806,700 BTC

On April 22, according to Lookonchain monitoring, BlackRock has been continuously increasing its BTC holdings recently, reaching 806,700 BTC (US$63.73 billion), a record high.

▌Aave Founder Responds to Recent Events: Recovers US$70 Million in ETH, Developing Multiple Solutions to Mitigate Risk

On April 23, Aave founder Stani Kulechov responded to recent events, stating that the team is continuously developing multiple solutions with the core objective of protecting user interests and restoring normal market order. He revealed that, with the collaboration of multiple parties, the Arbitrum Security Council has successfully recovered approximately US$70 million in ETH assets, which is expected to significantly reduce overall risk exposure.

... The team is currently in discussions with multiple partners, evaluating different handling paths, and expressing confidence in the final solution. Kulechov emphasized that the current focus is on resolving the issue rather than assigning blame, while continuously reviewing the incident and optimizing mechanisms. He stated that this incident is not only about Aave itself, but also a significant moment for the DeFi industry, and the team will continue to share updates with the public. On April 22, according to on-chain analyst Yu Jin, affected by the rsETH incident, funds continued to flow out of Aave. Aave's total deposits have decreased from $48.5 billion to $30.7 billion, with approximately $15.1 billion flowing out in three and a half days, representing nearly one-third of the total. Meanwhile, other major lending platforms showed mixed performance: Morpho: Total deposits decreased from $11.7 billion before the incident to $10.2 billion, with an outflow of approximately $1.5 billion. Spark: Its SparkLend business TVL rose from $1.9 billion before the event to $3.2 billion, a counter-trend increase of approximately $1.3 billion. Spark absorbed some of the whales and institutional funds withdrawn from Aave, including Justin Sun and a whale who bought in with $500 million in February.

▌USDC Treasury Mints 200 Million New USDC on Ethereum

On April 22, according to Whale Alert monitoring, at 22:00:23 Beijing time, USDC Treasury minted 200 million new USDC on Ethereum.

▌Coinbase to List Fluent (BLEND)

On April 23, Coinbase will list Fluent (BLEND) spot trading.

**Important Economic Developments** **▌Hassett Supports Powell's Explanation of Remaining as "Interim Fed Chair"** On April 23, White House National Economic Council Director Hassett expressed support for Federal Reserve Chairman Powell's plan—that he would remain as Fed chair temporarily if his successor was not confirmed by the Senate by the end of his term in May. Hassett stated on Wednesday, "I think this is the appropriate legal interpretation." Trump has nominated Warsh to succeed Powell, but Republicans currently do not have enough votes to move the nomination from the Senate Banking Committee to the Senate for confirmation. Republican Senator Thom Tillis indicated he would postpone the vote until the Justice Department ceases its alleged "sham" investigation into cost overruns in the Fed building renovation project. Hassett, speaking about Walsh, said, "We are very confident that he will become chairman in the appropriate time. I believe there will be discussions about how to proceed." (Jin Shi)

▌Iranian Parliament Speaker: Reopening the Strait of Hormuz is "Impossible" Given Such a Blatant Violation of the Ceasefire

On April 23, Iranian Parliament Speaker Ghalibaf stated that a comprehensive ceasefire is only meaningful if it is not violated by maritime blockade and acts of "hijacking the world economy." Reopening the Strait of Hormuz is impossible given such a blatant violation of the ceasefire. They have failed to achieve their goals through military aggression, and they will never succeed through bullying. The only way out is to recognize the rights of the Iranian nation.

...

▌Pakistani Officials: Positive Signals Received from Iran to Resume Face-to-Face Talks

On Wednesday, April 23, Pakistani officials remained optimistic that it was still possible to bring the US and Iran back to the negotiating table, despite Iran's seizure of two cargo ships in the Strait of Hormuz creating a new obstacle to their efforts to broker a long-term peace. Pakistani Prime Minister Sharif met with the Iranian ambassador on Wednesday. A Pakistani official briefed on the talks said a second round of negotiations could be held in the coming days. Former Pakistani ambassadors to the US and UK, Maleha Lodi, who is familiar with the current diplomatic efforts, said: "The ceasefire has opened up space for the resumption of diplomatic channels, and Pakistan believes that space is sufficient. Neither side has rejected negotiations." Another Pakistani official, briefed on the negotiations and requesting anonymity, said that following the extension of the ceasefire, Pakistani mediators have received positive signals from Iran expressing a desire to resume face-to-face talks.

... The official did not elaborate on what these signals were, nor did he specify whether Iran had told Pakistani mediators that lifting the U.S. blockade of the Strait of Hormuz was a prerequisite for participating in the second round of negotiations. (The New York Times) Trump: "Good News" for Iran Talks as Early as Friday April 22 – According to the New York Post, U.S. President Trump and Pakistani sources revealed on Wednesday that "good news" regarding a second round of U.S.-Iran negotiations could emerge as early as Friday. Pakistani sources said that positive mediation efforts with Iran have revived the possibility of more peace talks within the next "36 to 72 hours." When asked about this possible breakthrough, Trump replied via text message: "Possibly!" Previously, Trump had stated that he would extend the ceasefire agreement until the Iranian leadership could present a "unified solution." In the following hours, Pakistan continued to communicate with Iran through diplomatic channels. A Pakistani source stated that this timeline was based on an assessment of the relevant work.

▌Hassett: Trump Hopes Warsh Will Take Office at the Fed Soon

On April 23, White House National Economic Council Director Hassett stated that Trump hopes Warsh will take office at the Federal Reserve as soon as possible; core inflation is declining significantly, and declining inflation should help the Fed normalize interest rates.

Golden Encyclopedia

▌Can Bitcoin Still Be Called "Digital Gold" Under Geopolitical Crises?

Every time there is a geopolitical crisis, gold prices rise, while Bitcoin prices plummet. After six tests, the claim of "digital gold" has never been confirmed by data. Countries hoard gold but exclude Bitcoin from their reserves. For investors, Bitcoin has an asymmetry: it falls with stocks but does not rise with stocks.

Three structural asymmetries prevent Bitcoin from achieving safe-haven status: an oversupply of derivatives (market structure), the dominance of leveraged traders (participant composition), and a lack of repeatable behavioral records (behavioral accumulation). Bitcoin is not a safe-haven asset, but it is a "crisis-useful asset," capable of functioning effectively when borders are closed and banks fail. If these three asymmetries narrow, Bitcoin may no longer be a replica of gold, but rather a completely new "next-generation gold." Generational shifts and the widespread adoption of algorithms are key factors that could accelerate this process.

data.title: Federal Reserve Chairman's portfolio
data.description:
Article author:Thejaswini M A

Article compilation：Block unicorn

What Money Tells Us

Kevin Warsh believes that cryptocurrency is the next layer of financial infrastructure, built on top of protocols that process real transactions, and what the people who build these protocols are doing, in some ways, very much like fintech.

Throughout the disclosures, the closest thing to a speculative investment is Friends With Benefits, a social token community. Considering everything else in the portfolio, it's more like someone invited him to dinner, and he politely wrote a small check.

His public statements are consistent with this.

In 2011, Walsh saw the Bitcoin white paper at a dinner hosted by Marc Andreessen. In 2018, he wrote in the Wall Street Journal that Bitcoin could become a sustainable store of value, similar to gold. In 2021, he stated on CNBC that Bitcoin is the new gold for people under 40. In 2025, at the Hoover Institution, he made his most comprehensive statement to date: Bitcoin will not replace the dollar, but it can be an excellent regulator of monetary policy. I wouldn't call Walsh a Bitcoin extremist. A monetary policy expert who sees Bitcoin as a reliable indicator of the dollar's health may be more valuable to the industry in some ways.

Wash received $10.2 million in advisory fees from the Duken family office (Stanley Druckenmiller's investment arm). Druckenmiller is one of the world's most reputable macro investors and one of the few top investors who seriously discuss cryptocurrencies. Last month, Druckenmiller told Morgan Stanley that stablecoins will become the entire U.S. payment system within 10 to 15 years. In the same interview, he also called other cryptocurrencies "solutions proposed to find problems." Warsh's cryptocurrency investments align with the investment network of one of today's most reputable macro investors. Druckenmiller believes stablecoins represent the future and everything else is noise, while Warsh's portfolio is filled with the infrastructure that underpins stablecoins, suggesting a shared investment philosophy. Warsh has pledged to divest everything. Selling holdings of liquid tokens is relatively straightforward. However, unwinding limited partner (LP) interests in Polychain Capital or venture capital funds' holdings in illiquid early-stage companies is far more complex. Under OGE regulations, some funds with multiple LPs typically do not require divestment. However, OGE certification officials specifically cited the Juggernaut Fund's holdings, making a full divestment a condition for compliance. Due to previously signed confidentiality agreements, the underlying assets of these holdings remain undisclosed. Federal ethical guidelines typically require a one-year cooling-off period for matters that directly impact near-term economic interests. This rule may be particularly important as the Federal Reserve weighs its research on stablecoin legislation, tokenized deposits and securities, and central bank digital currencies (CBDCs). Consider what this means in practice. Congress is currently actively debating a stablecoin framework. The CLARITY Act is still under consideration in the Senate. Banks are conducting pilot programs for tokenized deposits. The Federal Reserve plays a consistent role in all of these areas. And the Federal Reserve Chairman, a true investor in decentralized finance (DeFi) protocols, Bitcoin payment infrastructure, and prediction markets, may have to stand by for the first year, watching his colleagues, who have never used a crypto wallet, offer their opinions. It's difficult to discuss Warsh's cryptocurrency holdings without considering the context of his nomination. Even before any cryptocurrency-related legislation was signed, the Trump family's cryptocurrency investments had already generated substantial profits. According to the Wall Street Journal, World Liberty Financial, a DeFi project listed as a "DeFi visionary" by Baron Trump on his website, had generated at least $1.2 billion in actual profits for the Trump family by early 2026. Meanwhile, while the president allowed 401(k) accounts to invest in cryptocurrencies, his family members were simultaneously selling off their cryptocurrency investments. Warsh's holdings were small-scale venture investments through a fund structure. The Trump family's holdings, on the other hand, were heavily concentrated in projects directly benefiting from federal policy. The two are vastly different in scale and structure. What they have in common is that this president, who benefited from the Federal Reserve's pro-cryptocurrency policy, chose a Federal Reserve chairman nominee with economic interests in the cryptocurrency industry, the very industry he would be overseeing. Whether this represents blatant corruption, strategic alignment of interests, or simply a natural evolution of the American system depends on one's perspective. This perspective often depends on whether you hold the relevant assets. Senator Tom Tillis stated that he will oppose any nominee for Federal Reserve Chair until the Justice Department's investigation into Powell concludes fully and transparently. The committee is partisan, meaning any defection by a Republican could delay confirmation. Powell's term as Fed Chair ends on May 15th. If Warsh fails to secure confirmation before then, Powell will continue as interim chair amidst oil prices above $100 per barrel, the unresolved situation in Iran, and markets still trying to determine if the Fed has a leader. For the cryptocurrency community, Warsh's revelations are a classic bittersweet joke. The good news? We finally have a Fed Chair who truly understands the difference between liquidity pools and swimming pools. The bad news? Because of his deep understanding of how cryptocurrencies work, federal ethics laws effectively landed him in a year-long "digital corner," forcing him to watch helplessly as his "anti-cryptocurrency" colleagues attempted to regulate a technology they might see as a sequel to *The Matrix*. These two things can coexist. He has a profound understanding of DeFi. He also believes that the Fed's primary task is monetary stability, and that asset price inflation is a sign of policy failure. These views will make the FOMC meeting very interesting. Warsh's disclosure is less a signal of institutional change and more a tacit acknowledgment of path dependence. Our past choices and technological evolution inevitably constrain the future. For decades, the Federal Reserve has followed a traditional operating model. As the monetary architecture shifts from paper money to negotiated currencies, the "path" naturally broadens. This is a slow and steady process of integrating new competencies into the existing system. It means that people are gradually realizing that to control the future of the dollar, they must first understand the language that will shape the future.

data.title: Cook's retirement and Truss's succession: The gap and reboot of Apple's trillion-dollar empire
data.description:
Author: 137Labs
