Just now, Tim Cook officially announced his resignation as CEO, a piece of news that quickly swept the global tech world. Since taking the reins from Steve Jobs in 2011, Cook has spent fifteen years propelling Apple from a tech company with a market capitalization of approximately $350 billion to a historical high approaching $4 trillion.

This is an almost undisputed business legend. However, the end of a legend often means the beginning of new uncertainties. According to the plan, Cook will officially step down as CEO this September, becoming Executive Chairman, and taking over this position is 50-year-old John Tenus—a "pure-blooded engineer" who grew up within Apple.

After the news was released, the entire industry reacted quickly, with even tech leaders including Sam Altman publicly paying tribute, calling Cook "a symbol of an era."

But beyond the tribute, a more pressing question has emerged: in the current era of explosive growth in artificial intelligence, has Apple fallen behind? I. The "Chosen Successor": A Pre-Rehearsed Power Transfer In fact, Tenus's rise to power was not a spur-of-the-moment decision, but rather a natural outcome of long-term planning. Speculation about his succession had been rife over the past year, and now that it's official, it simply confirms market expectations. From the board's perspective, this choice possesses a high degree of "certainty." First, there's the age match. Tenus is currently 50 years old, very close to the age when Cook took over, meaning he has the potential for a full long-term leadership cycle—ten years or even longer. This stability over time is extremely valuable for a large company. Second, and more importantly, is his technical background. Unlike Cook, who excels in supply chain and operations, Tenus has dedicated almost his entire career to hardware engineering. From joining Apple in 2001 to leading core product lines like the iPhone and Mac, his career path almost perfectly overlaps with Apple's hardware ecosystem. This "engineer-turned-leader" is precisely the type of leader Apple needs most at this stage. Finally, there's the "visibility" of the power transition. In recent years, Cook has increasingly delegated more public appearances to Tenus—from new product launches to retail store openings, media interviews, and strategic communications—these symbolic actions, originally the CEO's responsibility, have gradually shifted to him. This is not merely a decentralization of responsibility, but a reshaping of public perception: Apple is actively shaping the image of its next leader. In other words, even before his formal appointment, Tenus has already, to some extent, "exercised some of the CEO's power." II. Organizational Reorganization: A Rebalancing of Apple's Internal Power Structure With the rise of Tenus, Apple's internal technological power structure has also changed. One of the most noteworthy aspects is the further strengthening of the hardware system. Johny Srouji, who has long been responsible for chip development, has succeeded Tenus as Chief Hardware Officer. This adjustment is significant. Over the past decade, Apple has built its core competitive advantage through its self-developed chips (Apple Silicon), and Srouji has been a key driver of this strategy. This means that Apple's future technological roadmap will be more focused on two dimensions: product engineering capabilities (represented by Tenus) and underlying computing capabilities (controlled by Srouji). The convergence of these two lines essentially serves one goal—to regain technological dominance. The problem is that while this structure might have been powerful enough in the traditional hardware era, it may not hold true in the AI ​​era. III. The Delayed Future: Cook's "AI Debt" If there's one truly unfinished task from the Cook era, the answer is almost undisputed: artificial intelligence. Back in 2018, Apple brought in John Giannandrea from Google in an attempt to systematically improve its AI capabilities, especially to revive Siri. However, years later, this project not only failed but gradually evolved into a case of organizational and strategic failure. In the past few years, Siri's repeated upgrade promises have been repeatedly delayed, from initial feature demonstrations to constantly postponed release dates, gradually eroding market trust. Meanwhile, the power structure within the AI ​​team was constantly fragmented, shifting from centralized management to multiple executives sharing responsibilities. This fragmented structure made it difficult for Apple to maintain a unified pace of technological advancement. More symbolically, Apple ultimately chose to partner with Google, leveraging its model capabilities to support its AI system. While this move may be pragmatic from a business perspective, it appears passive strategically: one of the world's most valuable technology companies relying on a competitor for core technologies. The root of the problem lies not entirely in technology, but in the organizational mechanism. Apple has long been known for its small-scale decision-making and strong control, a model that was extremely efficient in the hardware era, but may become a hindrance in the AI ​​era, which requires rapid trial and error and open collaboration. Therefore, what Tenus inherited was not a complete system, but an AI strategy that had not yet been successfully implemented. IV. The Test in the ASI Era: Apple's Meaning Is Being Redefined If we broaden our perspective further, we can see that Apple is currently facing not just "lagging behind in AI," but a deeper paradigm conflict. Over the past two decades, Apple's success has been built on a closed loop of "hardware + system + ecosystem." However, as Artificial General Intelligence (ASI) gradually becomes a reality, the core of the technology is shifting from the device itself to the intelligent capabilities themselves. In other words, what users truly rely on may no longer be the phone, but the intelligent system running on the device. Under this trend, Apple's strengths and weaknesses are simultaneously amplified. On the one hand, over two billion devices worldwide constitute an unparalleled distribution network, an entry point that no AI company can replicate; but on the other hand, this vast ecosystem also implies path dependence, making radical transformation difficult. On-device AI is seen as a key breakthrough for Apple, emphasizing privacy and local computing power, which aligns perfectly with Apple's consistent values. However, this path remains fraught with uncertainty: it could become a differentiating advantage, or it could lose its competitiveness due to limitations in capabilities. Therefore, many of Apple's current choices—including introducing external models, strengthening chip capabilities, and adjusting organizational structure—are essentially about finding a balance between ideals and reality. V. Time Window: A Shorter Countdown Than Expected From the outside, Tenus seems to have ample time to prove himself. But reality may be more pressing. The next crucial juncture is likely to be the upcoming Worldwide Developers Conference (WWDC). This stage is not only for product launches but also a window for Apple to explain its technological roadmap to the world. If Apple cannot provide a clear AI strategy and product direction in the short term, market confidence will quickly waver. In other words, this succession is not a long-term issue, but rather a short-term stress test. Conclusion: On the surface, Cook's departure and Tenus's succession appear to be a smooth, orderly, and well-planned transfer of power; however, at a deeper level, it is actually a transition of eras without a definitive answer. Apple under Cook has maximized "commercial success"; while Apple under Tenus must answer a more difficult question: in a new world driven by artificial intelligence, can Apple once again become the company that "defines the future"? If Jobs created the soul of Apple, and Cook established its order, then Tenus's task may be to rediscover Apple's direction above that order. And this is the true significance of this power transition.

data.title: Encryption is essentially a bet on human progress.
data.description:
Author: Systematic Long Short Translator: Shan Ouba, Jinse Finance

Last week, my sister asked me the question I least wanted to answer in my career: "Can you teach me how to invest in cryptocurrency and get rich?" Most of the time, I politely avoid this topic, but since it was my sister, I agreed.

For the next few minutes, I explained to her the entry and exit channels for cryptocurrencies, centralized and decentralized exchanges, asset conversion, cross-chain bridges, different public chains, and other concepts. I told her that these are the foundation for enabling on-chain and off-chain fund flows.

She thanked me, perhaps with a hint of sarcasm, and then said she was satisfied with the steady returns from stocks and commodities. I finally escaped the awkward situation, but one thought lingered. This isn't the first time someone has felt helpless because they can't understand the workings of cryptocurrency.

It's not that it's truly obscure or difficult to understand, but the thought of conducting transactions solely through smart contracts without the backing of centralized institutions like banks is enough to deter many. I believe this "trust issue" is the core reason hindering widespread adoption of cryptocurrencies. However, I also firmly believe that cryptocurrencies, with their inherent value proposition, can transform global capital flows in a better way. Therefore, the "trust issue" urgently needs to be addressed. In this guest article, SysLS explores why the emergence of AI-based technologies is timely and poised to help cryptocurrencies fundamentally transform the financial world. Investing in cryptocurrencies is essentially betting that humanity will continue to pursue efficiency improvements. This seems like an inevitable gamble. I believe now is the optimal time because truly practical artificial intelligence has emerged, finally providing us with the tools to completely reshape how we interact with cryptocurrencies. This article will explain why cryptocurrencies will become a strategic asset class. Limited by the bottleneck of due diligence. Since the dawn of civilization, humans have pursued efficiency. We measure success by efficiency and are constantly striving to reduce friction in all kinds of transactions. Cross-border wire transfers used to take weeks; now they take seconds. Today, the bottleneck for most human processes is no longer technology, but the need for verification. Job hunting, opening a bank account, raising funds, etc., often still take weeks. This is not because of a lack of technology to quickly complete these processes, but because the verification work must be done by humans. Take the job hunting process as an example: employers need to verify that you are not an "inferior candidate." Even if you have previous work experience, employers still need to conduct independent verification because previous employers are not held liable if your performance was poor. Furthermore, some argue that top performers retain employment, and different work environments and responsibilities mean that past performance may not predict future results. Therefore, employers incur due diligence costs through multiple rounds of interviews and written tests. This process is time-consuming and labor-intensive—cumbersome, lacking standardization, and with unpredictable outcomes. This is why the recruitment process depends on both ability and luck (such as whether the interviewer is in a good mood before meeting you, or has just had a heated argument with their spouse). Clearly, all costs in this process are essentially verification costs. The actual act of recruiting is quick and easy—requiring only an email and a digital signature. Theoretically, if a "magic scoring tool" existed that could accurately assess a candidate's performance across all the dimensions you focus on, and you highly trust its scores, then the recruitment time would be reduced to the time it takes for the algorithm to screen scores, send employment contracts, and complete signing. Building a Trust Chain Effect: The reason why "magic scoring tools" can achieve near-instant recruitment is that you delegate the verification responsibility to the tool and gradually build trust in it. In reality, such scenarios are commonplace. Potential employers in the same industry often refer to the due diligence results of previous employers—if you graduated from a top university and have outstanding experience, the probability of your resume being directly discarded will be greatly reduced. Whenever possible, humans will transfer the verification cost to higher-level entities and believe that they will complete the necessary verification work for their own benefit. For example, most people choose to directly trust the review of government financial regulatory agencies when opening a bank account, rather than spending a lot of time, energy, and money on comprehensive due diligence of the bank. If we fully trust the government and its enforcement capabilities, we will trust its judgment on the solvency and safety of banks. This "chain of trust effect" is ubiquitous, permeating all aspects of life. Startups typically lack brand reputation and struggle to gain natural trust, but investments from well-known and successful large venture capital (VC) firms essentially endorse them. Even if the public is unfamiliar with the startup, they will choose to believe it if they trust the VC behind it, as they are entrusting the verification work to the VC's due diligence process. The Dilemma of Cryptocurrencies Traditionally, cryptocurrencies lack a "higher-level entity" to delegate verification costs. Because there is no higher entity to arbitrate honest mistakes—such as exchanging $50 million worth of USDC for $5,000 worth of tokens, or even sending them to the wrong address—the verification costs are borne entirely by the user. This problem is real because smart contracts are not designed for humans to interpret in a second. This is also the core reason why most cryptocurrencies remain in a niche market. The cryptocurrency products that are truly widely adopted by the retail public are mostly simplified versions of smart contracts by large centralized institutions—such as Binance and Coinbase. These retail users may not understand how Aave smart contracts work, but they trust Binance; therefore, Binance is able to build its business by simplifying the complexity of smart contracts and providing a user-friendly interface. This means that, from a technical perspective, ordinary cryptocurrency users actually only have two choices: wait for centralized institutions like Binance to release simplified versions (which require a fee), or interact directly with the protocol and bear the potentially huge risks. Although cryptocurrencies have developed to a relatively mature stage, their barrier to entry remains extremely high, and approving transactions is always unsettling—mainly because you know that once you make a mistake, there is no way to remedy it. Therefore, you will carefully check every transaction, but interpreting all the details of each transaction on the blockchain is already difficult, let alone deeply understanding the smart contracts you interact with. For those who understand software and its infinite combinability, the allure of cryptocurrency has always lay in its modular ecosystem of components—components that can interact with each other in a trustless environment guaranteed by algorithmic rules. The obstacle to this utopia lies in the edge cases of malicious actors and design flaws, forcing humans to bear the cost and friction of verifying each modular component. How can AI empower cryptocurrency? The rise of practical artificial intelligence allows us, for the first time, to delegate verification to a higher-level agent. This will fundamentally change how we interact with smart contracts. We will no longer need to personally verify the design and behavior of smart contracts, or even consciously understand the details of transactions. Instead, we will learn to trust a single agent—our intelligent agents (AI agents). This direction has shown enormous potential, with intelligent agents demonstrating exceptional capabilities in code understanding and logical reasoning. They can tirelessly parse and examine transactions in seconds, understanding the algorithmic rules followed by each smart contract. This means that, for the first time, we can realize the potential of cryptocurrencies through smart agents. If nine smart contracts need to interact in a single atomic transaction to achieve a goal most efficiently, simply have your smart agent verify that the transaction can achieve the expected result and that there is no malicious activity. This capability will become increasingly powerful as smart agents iterate. The Profound Significance of Algorithm Verification If we can solve the verification problem of smart contracts and the transactions they generate, we can move towards an ideal state: all types of interactions can be implemented in a permissionless, trustless environment guaranteed by algorithmic rules. If the verification cost of smart contracts drops to zero, then we can enjoy all the verification advantages and security guarantees brought by smart contracts for free. The core result is that all processes in this model will run at computational speed. For a civilization committed to faster development, reduced process friction, and improved efficiency, it is difficult to deviate from this model. It's natural that more and more goods and services are equipped with smart contract interfaces, clearly defining the rights you will gain and the costs you will incur. At the same time, this model has a self-compounding effect—each successful product becomes a composable module, empowering subsequent products. For example, a decentralized exchange has built a reputation system based on trader professionalism. Those wishing to hire traders through trustless job agreements can screen them based on reputation contracts from another unrelated decentralized exchange, while lending protocols can use this reputation data as one dimension of credit scoring. This model is not only extremely efficient—all processes and transactions proceed at computational speed—but also infinitely scalable, becoming increasingly powerful and unlocking more possibilities with increased participation and time. This is a positive compounding model. Therefore, if you believe that humanity will continue to move towards the "least effort path," pursuing solutions that allow us to accomplish more tasks faster, more efficiently, and in greater numbers, then the era of cryptocurrency has arrived.

data.title: Crypto VCs have shrunk across the board, so why did this firm still grow by 150%?
data.description: Amidst the overall decline of the crypto market, almost all crypto VCs are experiencing a shrinking of their assets under management (AUM), with the industry entering a brutal shakeout cycle. However, against this backdrop, a crypto venture capital fund less than five years old stands out as a notable exception, forging an independent growth trajectory amidst the contraction. According to a batch of unpublished crypto VC financial disclosure documents obtained by Fortune magazine last week from the U.S. SEC, data shows that the AUM of leading institutions such as Paradigm, Pantera, a16z crypto, and Multicoin all shrank in 2025, with Multicoin shrinking by more than half. The only exception is Haun Ventures, a venture capital fund established only four years ago, whose AUM has grown from an initial $1 billion to $2.5 billion in 2025. In the dismal crypto market, this is no accident. Last year, market news indicated that Haun Ventures was close to completing a new $1 billion fund-raising round. These developments reflect that Haun Ventures and its founder, Katie Haun, have passed the test of the market and LPs with their unique investment strategy, moving towards becoming a top-tier VC. Distinctive Founder Style and Background Haun Ventures' uniqueness was foreshadowed from the beginning. Founder Katie Haun is not a typical crypto investor. She served as a U.S. federal prosecutor for over a decade, investigating financial crimes, and created the U.S. government's first cryptocurrency task force. In 2018, she became a16z's first female partner and co-led the fund's cryptocurrency fund. She also served on the Coinbase board, thus possessing policy insight, institutional resources, and practical experience. This experience allows her to understand the crypto industry not simply from the perspective of "technological potential" or "market size," but from the perspective of compliance boundaries, systemic risks, and institutional embedding capabilities. The impact of this background is not immediately apparent, but it is profound. It's worth noting that Haun Ventures is one of only two funds in the crypto industry named after its founder, reflecting its distinct founder-led style. The other VC named after its founder is a16z. In its early days, Haun Ventures was not without its flaws, also navigating the pitfalls of market hype. Looking back at its investment history, Haun Ventures once prioritized NFTs, investing in at least four NFT projects in the first half of 2022, including Opensea, Autograph, ZORA, and Highlight. However, as the bubble in NFTs and related concepts rapidly burst, Haun Ventures demonstrated exceptional error correction and iteration capabilities, rapidly scaling back its operations from the second half of 2022, significantly reducing its investment frequency to respond with extreme caution to the market downturn. According to RootData, in the second half of 2022 and the entire year of 2023, Haun Ventures publicly disclosed participation in only six funding rounds, averaging one investment every three months. In an interview in June 2023, Rosenblum, a partner at Haun Ventures, stated that the company's investments were almost evenly split between digital tokens and traditional equity, and that approximately 30% of its funds had been invested in about twenty projects, including publicly traded, highly liquid tokens. These tokens included well-known cryptocurrencies such as Bitcoin and Ethereum, as well as small-cap tokens associated with specific projects. At that time, the price of Bitcoin hovered between $15,000 and $30,000 for an extended period, before peaking at $126,000 in 2025. This generated substantial investment returns for Haun Ventures, largely offsetting losses in areas such as NFTs and becoming a crucial cornerstone of its growth. Key Shift Starting in 2024, Haun Ventures' investment strategy began to shift significantly, focusing on B2B solution companies such as payment and developer platforms. At the time, these areas weren't particularly exciting; they lacked a compelling narrative and were unlikely to generate market sentiment quickly. However, they happened to coincide with a crucial juncture in the industry's transition from speculation to practical application. That year, Haun Ventures invested in nearly ten B2B companies, including stablecoin payment platform Bridge, crypto-native infrastructure platform Conduit, crypto protocol economic security solution Chaos Labs, Solana development platform Helius, and crypto payment platform BVNK. In terms of investment style, Haun Ventures particularly favored leading investments. According to RootData statistics, Haun Ventures led 22 out of 39 publicly participated funding rounds, a lead investment rate exceeding 56%, ranking first among top-tier VCs. This demonstrates Haun Ventures' exceptional conviction in its portfolio and its willingness to support high-potential early-stage projects with substantial funding. Currently, payments have become the sector with the highest valuation premium and the clearest exit path in the crypto space. Haun Ventures' early positioning, coupled with its consistent lead investment style, has resulted in very substantial investment exit returns. Since October 2024, more than five of Haun Ventures' portfolio companies have been acquired, with several payment companies achieving high multiple returns. For example, Haun Ventures led a $200 million investment in the stablecoin development platform Bridge, which was eventually acquired at a valuation exceeding $1.1 billion. Haun Ventures led a $750 million investment in the crypto payment platform BVNK, which was eventually acquired for over $1.8 billion. In an environment where exit channels for crypto assets are increasingly narrowing and secondary market liquidity is highly concentrated in the hands of leading companies, Haun Ventures proved the viability of another path: investing in companies that solve real payment pain points and have compatibility with traditional finance through equity investments, achieving high-multiple exits through mergers and acquisitions, is more capital-efficient than holding a bunch of highly illiquid tokens. From chasing the trend of NFTs to a balanced allocation of tokens and equity, and then focusing on B2B payments and infrastructure, Haun Ventures' evolutionary path is a microcosm of the shift in crypto VC from a speculative to a value-oriented approach. Katie Haun's compliance background, the fund's ability to quickly correct mistakes, its prudent investment pace, and its high lead investment ratio strategy, combined with its accurate grasp of real-world applications and exit paths, have collectively built a moat that allows it to weather economic cycles. As the industry bubble burst, institutions that expanded through storytelling and leverage shrank significantly. Haun Ventures, with its foundation of compliance and stability, emerged as the most certain winner in the crypto winter, pointing the way for survival and growth in the next phase for the entire VC industry.

data.title: Ethereum's meme season is back.
data.description:
This round of Ethereum memes started with a puppy and a reply from Musk. A few days ago, SpaceX founder Elon Musk replied to a post by media personality Glenn Beck on SpaceX. The post said that a teenage girl, before dying of cancer, designed a Shiba Inu plush toy named Asteroid and sent it on the 2024 SpaceX Polaris Dawn mission. The plush toy served as a zero-gravity indicator on the spacecraft, the first thing that would float when a human enters a state of weightlessness. One of the girl's last wishes was for Asteroid to become the official mascot of SpaceX. Musk's reply was only four words: Will answer shortly. On-chain traders, with their keen instincts, acted immediately. They found a memecoin on Ethereum called $ASTEROID, which had existed for 19 months and was virtually ignored. But that day, it surged by over 1,000% in six hours. Someone invested 1 ETH and withdrew $470,000 three hours later. This get-rich-quick story spread rapidly on social media, triggering a new wave of FOMO. Ethereum mainnet gas fees subsequently climbed from 0.052 Gwei, stabilizing around 0.6 Gwei for several days, a more than tenfold increase. The number of trading pairs on Uniswap V2 began to surge, and the 24-hour trading volume of the meme sector briefly surpassed that of mainstream DeFi protocols. Gas fees are a good barometer. It's telling us: Ethereum's meme season is back. Today, let's examine the characteristics of this batch of Ethereum memes and their respective narrative logic. The $ASTEROID dog is popular not only because Musk mentioned it, but also because it has a "real physical existence": it actually flew into space, there are photos, mission records, and it can be verified. This is different from ordinary memes that are simply created out of thin air; it has a real-world anchor. This logic subsequently spawned a batch of new projects themed around "real-world mascot characters": For example, $RISE flies the NASA flag, describing itself as the "official NASA mascot." Of course, NASA hasn't authorized any tokens; this is a standard "riding on the coattails of an official image" tactic. But the narrative is clear enough: space agency + American symbol + the subsequent association with $ASTEROID. Within days of its launch, its market capitalization exceeded $900,000, making it the most liquid project in this wave of space-themed projects.

FLOAT directly reuses the core prop of ASTEROID: the zero-gravity indicator. The project is called "SpaceX Zero-G Squad," and the website is floatsquad.xyz. The logic is to turn the ritual represented by ASTEROID (dropping a doll into the spacecraft at each launch to confirm the zero-gravity state) into a collective narrative. It saw a 24-hour increase of over 2000%, but its size is extremely small, and it is currently undergoing a correction.

...

Before each launch, a doll is dropped to confirm the weightless state.

There's an outlier in the space narrative. $CLUTCH doesn't follow the space route, but instead piggybacks on another upcoming real-world event: the FIFA World Cup, which will open on June 11, 2026. Clutch is the official mascot of the US team released by FIFA, a bald eagle wearing the number 10 jersey.

The $CLUTCH project team directly filled in the URL of FIFA's mascot page in the website's section, a blatant display of arrogance. Clearly, this meme is betting on a "calendar catalyst," with external events continuously driving traffic as the tournament approaches.

The stock price surged over 43,000% in 24 hours, but its market capitalization is still less than $700,000, placing it in its very early stages. Besides the mascot concept, the $ASTEROID dog has reignited the Musk and Tesla concepts, such as $RIZO. Rizo's narrative revolves around a hedgehog, originally created in 2008 by the Spanish insurance company Génesis Seguros as their corporate mascot. The hedgehog, making an "OK" sign with a friendly expression, was initially just commercial material. However, around 2013, netizens widely disseminated it as the "haha yes" meme series—paired with various affirmative captions—becoming a universal reaction image expressing "this is right" or "I'm satisfied." Musk incorporated the hedgehog into Tesla's product experience in 2019: it appeared on the Model Y purchase confirmation page with the caption "S3XY". In the following years, Rizo appeared in the subtle design of limited-edition Tesla Cyber ​​Beer bottles, an Easter egg on the Gigafactory Texas flagpole (only visible with a drone view), a cyberpunk version of the Cybertruck purchase confirmation page, and on official Tesla T-shirts. This is a meme symbol that Musk himself has consistently and repeatedly confirmed, not just a fan interpretation. The logic behind the $RIZO memecoin is built on this relationship. Currently, its market capitalization is close to $200,000, and it has rebounded by over 28% in the past hour. The comic book industry is rife with IP-generated memes. Pepe the Frog's Brother, Mystery. [Image of Pepe's creator, Matt Furie] Matt Furie, the creator of Pepe the Frog, published his first book, *The Night Riders*, in 1999. This wordless picture book features four animal characters: a frog, a mouse, a dragon, and a bat. For many years, no one knew the frog's name until someone found a note at the end of the book revealing it was called Mystery.

The main animal image of The Night Riders

In Furie's own NFT series HEDZ, there is also a character named Mystery, who is his avatar wearing an orange hood—in a sense, it is the author's own declaration of identification with this character.

The narrative framework given by the $MYSTERY community is only one sentence: "You missed PEPE, this is your second chance."

This sentence resonates in the crypto community not because it is logically sound, but because everyone who experienced the PEPE surge remembers the feeling of "not daring to buy." This fear was precisely invoked. $MYSTERY's marketing team has partnered with the team behind Brett (whose current market capitalization is approximately $2 billion), providing some endorsement. Currently valued at nearly $1.9 million, it's one of the most liquid projects among these new ones, with over a million dollars in trading volume in 24 hours.

FLORK and its derivative universe

Among all these new projects, $FLORK is also a meme that, despite having no direct connection to crypto, has experienced the largest price surge in a short period. It saw a nearly 6,000% increase in 6 hours, with over $8 million in trading volume in 24 hours.

Flork of Cows is a webcomic that began updating in 2012, created by Brian DiAntonio. The art style is extremely rudimentary, with abstract figures in an MS Paint style, resembling unfinished sock dolls, and the expressions and dialogue follow an existentialist, everyday satirical style.

Flork of Cows is a webcomic that has been updated since 2012, created by Brian DiAntonio. The art style is extremely rudimentary, with abstract little figures in an MS Paint style, looking like unfinished sock dolls, and the expressions and dialogue follow an existentialist, everyday satirical style.

Flork of Cows is a webcomic that began updating in 2012, created by Brian DiAntonio. The art style is extremely rudimentary, with abstract figures resembling unfinished sock dolls, and the expressions and dialogue follow an existentialist, everyday satirical style.

That "low-cost but incredibly addictive" vibe is similar to early Rage Comics and Trollface, but it has outlasted them because Flork's content is universal; people from any cultural background can recognize themselves in those absurd little characters. It's especially popular in Latin America, becoming part of the everyday emotional language of the Spanish-speaking internet. The Ethereum version of $FLORK's contract was created back in April 2023, dormant for three years, and then launched. With a market capitalization approaching $10 million, it's a major player among these new projects. Its explosion subsequently spurred the expansion of the "Flork universe." $FLORKY is the female version of Flork that just launched today. A female character occasionally appearing in the Flork of Cows comics saw a 1331% increase in 6 hours and even opened an Instagram account. $BABYFLORK, the baby version, saw a 1722% increase in 24 hours. This "mainstream → derivative baby/girl" path has been followed by most major IPs, representing a highly mature expansion logic in the meme sector. Political memes, MAGA variants. If mascot concepts and space narratives are driven by emotion, political memes follow a different logic: a sense of opposition and identity. $MAGA, short for Make Aliens Great Again, is a variation of Trump's campaign slogan, recently grafted onto UFO/alien narratives. This isn't a random compilation: the US government did indeed begin systematically releasing UAP (Unidentified Aerial Phenomena) files in 2025, an event that highly overlaps with conspiracy theories, the Tucker Carlson audience, and the MAGA political symbol within the English-speaking crypto community. The meme's official website directly links to aliens.gov, the US government's official UAP information disclosure page. This "direct reference to real official assets" is the same narrative technique used by $CLUTCH (linking to the FIFA website) and $RISE (using NASA's image). Additionally, there's $BRITAIN, a meme that follows a British version of the MAGA route, set against the backdrop of the unexpected rise of Nigel Farage's Reform UK party in the 2024 general election. "Restore Britain" is a real political slogan. The meme's TikTok account has reached out to right-wing political audiences outside the crypto community. Its 24-hour increase of 220% is relatively mild, but its stability is higher than other very new projects in this batch. It has been online for several days, with balanced buying and selling, showing signs of continued operation. However, the risk of political memes lies in their fixed audience base and weaker ability to expand beyond their core audience compared to purely cultural memes. But their community cohesion is often stronger, making them less prone to rapid disintegration due to market downturns. One observation: This is very different from Solana's game. The community pointed this out in a tweet on April 18th: Solana memes are PvP: quick in and out, mainly trading between traders, with on-chain lifecycles measured in hours. Ethereum memes are different; they're slower but tend to accumulate more narrative density. PEPE built a community on Ethereum that has lasted for years, and SHIB has carved out its own Layer 2 presence on Ethereum. This round of Ethereum meme activation, technically speaking, coincided with a specific window: after EIP-4844, gas is no longer a barrier, but the diversion of L2 to mainnet culture also means that traffic on the mainnet is exceptionally scarce. Once something truly popular emerges, the focus of funding will be stronger than ever before. Most of these memes will eventually disappear, but these narratives and perspectives are still very memorable. Note: The tokens mentioned above are purely community-driven speculative assets, without audits or roadmaps, and are used only for case analysis purposes. They do not constitute any investment advice.

data.title: From IDE assistant to productivity powerhouse, why is SpaceX investing heavily in Cursor?
data.description:
Author: BruceBlue

SpaceX officially announced a deep partnership with Cursor (with an option to acquire it for $60 billion by the end of this year). My first reaction upon seeing the news was: Holy crap, this is really going to change the world.

Even more coincidentally, just yesterday, GitHub Copilot suddenly removed the entire Claude Opus series from its platform. As a result, the only plan on the market that still allows stable Opus usage without KYC is Cursor Pro.

I'm both a Cursor Pro user and an X Premium+ user, and I recently used Composer 2.5 extensively again. Considering SpaceX's move, I'm writing down my honest feelings and judgments so you can see if it's worth it.

Regarding this acquisition, what exactly is SpaceX after? Many people think @elonmusk is just "rich and willful," but after reviewing it, the motivation is actually very clear:

What is SpaceX really after?

Acquiring users + securing an ecosystem entry point: Cursor is already the default IDE for some developers (especially in AI), with extremely high-quality users and good payment and renewal rates. By acquiring this entry point, SpaceX is essentially bringing top global developers into Musk's vast ecosystem.

Completing the final piece of Grok: Grok's biggest weakness right now isn't computing power (Colossus is already very powerful), but rather real-world use cases and a feedback loop.

Completing Grok's final piece:

... Cursor provides massive amounts of high-quality code data and real developer workflows, which is crucial for training the next generation of coding models. This accelerates all internal software projects: Starship, FSD, Optimus, Starlink… these projects have massive codebases and iterate extremely rapidly. With the deep integration of Cursor and Grok, SpaceX's internal development efficiency can reach a new level. Laying out "Physical World AI": Musk ultimately wants to create AI that can control real hardware. If Cursor can be used for mobile applications, CLIs, or even directly generate code deployable to Starship/Optimus, the entire chain will be complete. In short, this is not a simple acquisition, but an ecosystem-level integration of "AI + Physical World". What will happen in the next 6-18 months? Based on my experience using it recently, here are a few of the most likely directions for its implementation: Deep embedding of Grok into Cursor (most certain): In the future, the latest Grok versions, including those with real-time X data, will be directly accessible within Cursor. The latest papers, vulnerabilities, and framework discussions you see on X can be fed into the model with a single click, further improving the accuracy of generated code. Full-scale explosion of Multi-Agent capabilities: Cursor itself already supports multi-agent, and I feel that Composer 2.5 is significantly better than before. In the future, we may see scenarios where "one agent writes code, one agent runs tests, one agent performs security audits, and one agent generates documentation," truly realizing an "AI team." From IDE to "Full-Scenario Development Platform": I've recently noticed a clear shift in Cursor's focus towards mobile (the web version already offers many features). It's highly likely that a mobile app (for reviewing/editing code anytime, anywhere) will be released soon, along with a CLI version (for direct use on servers and CI/CD environments). It might even directly integrate with Starlink/Optimus hardware. Hidden benefits for X Premium+ users: It's predicted that within 3-6 months, Premium+ will likely offer Cursor credit or joint discounts. Dual-subscription users are most likely to benefit from this "one subscription for all" advantage. If the acquisition goes through for $60 billion, Cursor will become the "official development tool" of the Musk ecosystem. Your code will likely be able to directly access SpaceX's real flight data, Tesla's hardware interfaces, and even Optimus's motion planning library. This will create a significant competitive advantage for developers working on hardcore projects. Why I strongly recommend Cursor now: I've used Copilot, Anti-gravity, Codex, etc. before, but since Copilot discontinued Opus, I've completely switched my main alternative to Cursor Pro. My honest impressions after using it: Composer 2.5 is indeed stronger: context understanding, long file processing, and complex refactoring capabilities have all been significantly improved. Multi-Agent really works: I tried having it run 3-4 agents simultaneously to collaborate on a medium-sized module, and the success rate was much higher than I expected. Currently, it's the only decent way to "get free Opus": no KYC, no queuing, and no worries about credit limits—this is especially valuable after Copilot cut Opus. The mobile trend is already very clear: the web version experience is getting better and better, and a native mobile app + CLI will most likely be released soon, which is incredibly convenient for people who are frequently on the go and want to code anytime. In short, Cursor is no longer just an "AI-assisted tool," but a "productivity platform that can truly replace some development work."
